summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Application by the corporate entities for indemnity costs of the property division proceedings – Where the wife was wholly unsuccessful against the applicants – Consideration of costs of third parties – Where the wife’s conduct of her case was inefficient – Arrangement between the applicants and the husband affecting the property capable of division – Applicants’ failure to call evidence of the arrangement – Where the applicants produced unacceptable obfuscations – Costs order would not be just – Application dismissed.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALDRIDGE J:

On 25 July 2025 I made property settlement orders between Ms Ismailova (“the wife”) and Mr Tanzi (“the husband”) and dismissed the claims made by the wife against the present applicants (the fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, eighth and eleventh respondents in the substantive proceedings) (Tanzi & Ismailova [2025] FedCFamC1F 499). I incorporate those reasons into these.
The applicants seek an order that the wife pay their costs of the proceedings on an indemnity, or alternatively, ordinary basis. The wife opposes such an order and contends that if such an order is found to be justified, the order should be made against the husband or that he be required to indemnify her against it.
The proposed orders against the husband are permitted by s 117 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) which empowers the Court to make such orders as is just in all of the circumstances. See also Sanderson v Blyth Theatre Co [1903] 2 KB 533 and Nationwide News Pty Ltd v Naidu (No 2) [2008] NSWCA 71 at [15].
Section 117 governs the issues of costs in this matter as the hearing commenced before the repeal of that section (cl 15 of Sch 4 of the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth). See generally Navickas & Fried (No 2) (2025) FLC 94-248 at [18]–[19]).
The parties directed their submissions to s 114UB of the Act. No difficulty arises in that the provisions are substantially the same.
Under s 117, parties to family law proceedings are to bear his or her own costs unless the Court considers, in all of the circumstances, that a different order is just. In doing so, the Court must have regard to the matters raised in s 117(2A).
The applicants are third parties to the marriage. In such cases the Court is more inclined to make a costs order in their favour if the circumstances favour it (Burt & C Pty Ltd (In Liquidation) [2024] FedCFamC1A 203 at [7]; Wei & Xia (No 2) [2024] FedCFamC1A 138 at [35]; Conrad & Conrad (2020) 61 Fam LR 301 at [43]; E Pty Ltd & Zunino (No 2) [2020] FamCAFC 272 at [17]).
The financial circumstances of the parties are, essentially, as set out in the substantive reasons.
The wife received net property of $2,142,382 pursuant to the property division proceedings. The costs sought against her are approximately 16.5 per cent of that amount. She earns a modest salary from which she must support the parties’ two children without any assistance from the husband.
The financial circumstances of the applicants are not known.
The wife’s conduct of the proceedings was not exemplary. It took time for her to identify and plead her case. The Points of Claim filed on her behalf were difficult to understand and confusing. A sham was alleged but was not pursued at the hearing.
The applicants were critical of the wife’s failure to call a forensic accountant in her case. It is true that her case was poorly prepared, however, as I will explain shortly, that is not entirely her fault. Given the state of the accounts, again to be mentioned shortly, I am not sure what such an expert could have usefully added.
The wife was wholly unsuccessful against the applicants.
The wife did not succeed in her application for exclusive occupancy of the V Street property and vacant possession was ordered. It was necessary for the applicants to bring an Enforcement Application to remove her from that property, although she vacated the property prior to the application being heard. That favours a costs order, but the amount involved, in the scheme of this case, is small.
The Court is also to take into account any other relevant matter.
There was undoubtedly a business arrangement between the applicants and the husband. The details of it were known by the husband and Ms Q, and possibly no-one else, but certainly not the wife. The owner of the applicant entities, Ms Q’s mother, did not give evidence.
There was, no doubt, a substantial shift in the arrangement between the husband and the applicants which ended up with the husband no longer having the substantial capital interests in or held by the applicants. The bulk of the evidence, but not all, suggests that this was triggered by the separation of the husband and the wife and by her claim for a property settlement.
Again, the precise nature of the re-arrangement was known to the husband and Ms Q and not the wife. They each gave evidence as to what it was. That evidence was not easy to reconcile. Ultimately, I did not accept the evidence of either of them. I found that the accounts of the applicants were completely unsatisfactory in that that they were inconsistent, apparently illogical and could not be reconciled with other evidence. I found that they could not be relied upon at all, as is explained fully in the substantive reasons.
As the acceptable evidence did not disclose the contents of the re-arrangement, I was left to infer some sort of arrangement between them whereby the husband gave up, essentially, his capital interests in the applicants in return for an income stream. Of course, capital interests are capable of division but income that has been received and spent is not.
Whilst the applicants called their accountant, he did not give evidence as to the restructure, the accounts or the instructions he was given. That was so even after I re-listed the matter for further submissions on the reliability of the accounts.
In the substantive reasons I said:
I also found it difficult to accept the evidence of [Ms Q]. It was not consistent with the husband’s evidence or the accounts. On a couple of occasions, when posed difficult questions, [Ms Q] would respond with things such as “That would be a question for the accountant” (Transcript 25 September 2024, p.199 lines 10–11), or “I’m not an accountant... I can’t give evidence to what these figure correlate to without having direction from an accountant” (Transcript 25 September 2024, p.209 lines 9–11).
130 This was also a frequent retort by the husband on other aspects of the matter.
The accountants did not explain these matters. Whilst the current accountant was on affidavit, which annexed a summary of what he considered the current ownership structure to be, his evidence did not attempt to explain the accounts. He was not cross-examined. The true nature of the structure of [F] Pty Ltd and the [F] Unit Trust and payments to and from the accounts are particularly within the knowledge of the husband, [Ms Q] and the accountants. It would be expected that one or either of them would be able to call accounting evidence to bolster their case (Blatch v Archer (1774) 1 Cowper 63). It was not for counsel for the wife to cross-examine the accountant to seek out such evidence.
I was also critical of the letter of demand of 2021 sent to the husband saying:
Further, I do not regard the 2021 letter as a genuine letter of demand. The fact that a draft was sent first to the husband for his comments strongly suggests this to be the case. The terms of the letter and its timing sit oddly with the concerns raised in it. It casts a further shadow over the evidence of [Ms Q] and the husband. Its existence casts a doubt on the genuineness of the dilution of the husband’s unit holding.
Given the timing of the events and the letter of demand, the rejection of the evidence of Ms Q and the husband, and the apparent difficulty with the veracity of the accounts, the obvious inference was that the arrangement was made shortly after the wife made her claim in 2018. It worked to the clear detriment of the wife.
It is true that her claims under s 106B of the Act failed due to a number of matters discussed in the substantive reasons, one of which was the deficiencies in the pleadings. Another was the nature of the re-arrangement. The husband received a significant income stream from the applicants which, as far as could be seen, had been spent by him. That was in lieu of the capital interests given up by him. Restoration of the capital interest would have to be on the basis that the income was returned, which was not possible.
The applicants entered into some sort of arrangement with the husband which had the effect that the property capable of division between the husband and wife was diminished. The respondent then called unsatisfactory evidence and unreliable accounts in an attempt to explain it. The wife had to deal with that and expose the difficulties.
I take all these matters into account. I find the last to be most persuasive. Had the applicants produced clear, cogent and convincing evidence of what had occurred and accounts which supported it, that may well have been different. They did not. I do not consider that the producers of unacceptable obfuscations, whether intended or not, should receive a favourable costs order.
In all of the circumstances of this matter, I am not persuaded that a costs order is just. The application is dismissed.
Both the husband and the wife sought the payment of their costs of the costs application by their respective responses. This was not pressed in their written submissions. Nonetheless, I am not satisfied there is any reason to depart from the usual position and the parties will bear their own costs of the costs application.
